one_line_takeaway: CodeHacker is an LLM-agent pipeline that self-calibrates its judging tools and then generates targeted adversarial test cases to expose weak coverage in competitive-programming benchmarks, substantially raising true-negative rates (correctly rejecting buggy 'Accepted' solutions) and producing better RL training data -- a benchmark-quality/evaluation-rigor contribution, not an AI-security paper.
key_finding: On the 2,000-problem CodeContest+ subset, CodeContest++ (the paper's augmented dataset) reaches Overall VPR 100.00%, Special Judge TNR 96.05% and Traditional Judge TNR 96.31%, versus the CodeContest+ baseline's 84.04%/85.72% and CodeContests' 76.33%/77.69% (Table 1) -- i.e., substantially better rejection of incorrect solutions than prior datasets, at a modest cost in TPR (95.86% overall vs. baseline's inflated ~96-99%). An ablation on Special Judge problems (Table 5) shows the refined Checker alone raises TNR from 82.18% to 84.04%, while adding generated Hack Cases is the decisive step, lifting TNR to 96.05% (TPR 96.38%); interestingly, adding just the refined Validator slightly lowered TNR (82.18% to 82.08%) because it removed a 'masking effect' where invalid inputs had inadvertently caused incorrect solutions to fail. Across backbone models (Table 2), DeepSeek V3.2 achieved the highest HSR (64.83%) vs. GPT-5-Mini (51.40%) and Gemini-3.0-Flash-Preview (35.65%); disabling explicit reasoning collapsed DeepSeek V3.2's HSR to 23.76% (a 2.7x drop), framed as evidence that 'hacking is a reasoning task.' On CodeHackerBench, adjusted Pass@1 dropped across all evaluated models relative to standard scoring (Table 3), e.g., DeepSeek V3.2 83.3% to 81.6% (delta -1.7%), Qwen3-32B (non-thinking) 51.0% to 47.5% (delta -3.5%), with a reported rank reversal where Gemini-3.0-Flash, which beat GPT-5-Mini on original tests, is found less robust under the adversarial benchmark. Component ablation (Table 4, GPT-5-Mini backbone) shows the full agent reaches 51.40% HSR; removing the Refinement Loop is most damaging (46.60%), removing the Code Analyst gives 49.86%, removing Stress Test gives 50.12%, and removing the Anti-Hash Generator gives 51.00% (though it triggers on only ~0.4% of hash-using submissions and achieves 100% success rate there). RL-trained Qwen3-4B on CodeContests++ outperformed both the base model and a CodeContest+-trained variant on LiveCodeBench pass@5 across Easy/Medium/Hard difficulty (Figure 2: 98.5/80.9/27.6 vs. base 97/73.5/25 and CC+ 97/75/27).
core_contribution: CodeHacker is an LLM-agent framework that improves the reliability of competitive-programming (Codeforces-style) evaluation benchmarks by automatically generating adversarial test cases that expose incorrect solutions previously marked 'Accepted' by weak test suites. It has two phases: (1) an Evaluation Tool Calibration phase where the agent iteratively attacks and repairs its own Validator (input-constraint checker) and Checker (output-correctness checker) via self-generated adversarial probes (Bypass/Rejection attacks on the Validator, Deception/Rejection attacks on the Checker, with an anti-hallucination cross-verification pipeline and <5% human expert intervention for algorithmically hard verification logic); and (2) an Adversarial Case Generation phase where a 'Code Analyst' (using a dual C++ sandbox + Python interpreter execution interface) plans attacks executed by three generator strategies -- Stress Test (boundary/size maximization), LLM-based semantic generation, and an Anti-Hash Generator that formulates rolling-hash collision search as a Shortest Vector Problem solved via LLL lattice reduction (falling back to a birthday-attack search for smaller moduli/non-linear hashes). The authors also release CodeHackerBench, ~6,000 problem-submission pairs mined from discrepancies in CodeContest+, verified via a two-tier human protocol (100% validity on a 600-case expert sample), and CodeContests++, an augmented dataset built by applying the refined Validator/Checker plus generated hack cases to 2,000 CodeContest+ problems.
threat_model: Not applicable -- there is no security threat model in the paper. 'Adversarial' and 'hacking' are used in the competitive-programming sense (Codeforces 'hack' phase: a contestant submits a counterexample input that causes another contestant's accepted-looking solution to fail judging), targeting logical/algorithmic bugs (Wrong Answer, Runtime Error, Time Limit Exceeded, Memory Limit Exceeded) in programs solving algorithmic problems -- not exploits against real software systems, models, or infrastructure. The paper explicitly distinguishes this from classical ML adversarial attacks (small input perturbations), stating its method 'searches directly within the valid input space for new test cases that expose latent logical errors.' The Ethics Statement addresses only two non-security concerns: prohibiting unauthorized scraping of Codeforces/QOJ hack data, and mandating fully offline/local evaluation to avoid DoS load on public online judges.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: the agent is not infallible and may miss subtle algorithmic flaws, particularly performance-related bugs (time/memory limits); evaluation covers only a subset of programming languages (C++ only) and problem domains; human expert intervention (though described as <5% of cases and low-cost) is still required for high-difficulty problems (Codeforces rating > 2000) where validators/checkers require solving a secondary hard problem (e.g., graph isomorphism, Hamiltonian path existence); the framework's applicability to broader software-engineering settings (e.g., SWE-bench) is stated as 'conceptually transferable' but not empirically demonstrated in this paper.

datasets_used: CodeContest+ (2,000 randomly selected problems: 1,000 Traditional Judge, 1,000 Special Judge)
CodeContests (Li et al., 2022c) - baseline comparison dataset
HardTests (He et al., 2025) - baseline comparison dataset
TACO (Li et al., 2023b) - baseline comparison dataset
CodeHackerBench (new, ~6,000 problem-submission pairs mined from CodeContest+ discrepancies, human-verified)
CodeContest++ / CodeContests++ (new, augmented dataset built on the 2,000-problem CodeContest+ subset)
LiveCodeBench (287 AtCoder problems, used for RL evaluation via pass@1/pass@5)
quantitative_results: Table 1 VPR: CodeContests 71.41%, HardTests 97.32%, TACO 81.84%, CodeContest+ 99.66%, CodeContest++ (Ours) 100.00%
Table 1 Special Judge TNR: CodeContests 77.69%, HardTests 75.37%, TACO 85.48%, CodeContest+ 84.04%, CodeContest++ (Ours) 96.05%
Table 1 Traditional Judge TNR: CodeContests 76.33%, HardTests 79.25%, TACO 83.70%, CodeContest+ 85.72%, CodeContest++ (Ours) 96.31%

Table 2 HSR: DeepSeek V3.2 64.83%, GPT-5-Mini 51.40%, Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B 38.37%, Gemini-3.0-Flash-Preview 35.65%, Qwen3-32B 23.64%; non-thinking mode: DeepSeek V3.2* 23.76%, Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B* 20.06%, Qwen3-32B* 10.00%
Table 4 ablation HSR (GPT-5-Mini): Full 51.40%, w/o Code Analyst 49.86%, w/o Stress Test 50.12%, w/o Anti-Hash Generator 51.00%, w/o Refinement Loop 46.60%
Table 5 Special Judge pipeline ablation: Baseline TNR 82.18%/TPR 95.34%; + Refined Validator TNR 82.08%/TPR 95.50%; + Refined Checker TNR 84.04%/TPR 96.62%; + Hack Cases TNR 96.05%/TPR 96.38%
Figure 2 LiveCodeBench Pass@5 by difficulty (Easy/Medium/Hard): Base Model 97/73.5/25, RL w/ CC+ 97/75/27, RL w/ Ours 98.5/80.9/27.6
Anti-Hash Generator: triggered on ~0.4% of submissions using rolling hashes, achieved 100% success rate on those
baselines_compared: Qwen3-4B RL-trained on CodeContest+ (without hack-case augmentation)
